title: Add storage option to strip SUID & SGID bits from files as they are extracted from image layers
body:
## Overview

This pull request adds a new `strip_suid_sgid` storage option.  When set, the SUID and SGID permission bits are stripped (set to zero) in the mode of all filesystem objects as they are extracted from image layers.

Many application container images are built from base images that include system utilities that have these bits set.  Often these utilities are not actually used by those application containers.  In other cases, they are used by an entrypoint script that is running as root within the container.  In either case, the application container functions just fine without these bits set.  Of course, this is not true of all application container images, so every application must be tested to ensure that it works with this setting.

Given the potential to break applications, why would anyone want to use this setting?

First, leaving files with unneeded SUID and SGID permissions lying around is not great from a security POV, particularly if the image was not pulled by a rootless process.  Second, this setting allows image layers containing files with these bits set to be pulled by a systemd service that is running with `RestrictSUIDSGID=yes`.  Without this feature, an image which contains such files (even if they are removed by a higher layer within the image) cannot be pulled by such a service, because it is not allowed to create filesystem objects with these permission bits set.

## Commits

I have attempted to break this PR into digestible chunks.

* The first commit adds the option to the configuration file TOML and passes it down to the `TarOptions` struct (`OptionsConfig` → `StoreOptions` → `store` → `layerStore` → `ApplyDiffOpts` → `TarOptions`!).  `ApplyDiffOpts.StripSUIDSGID` is a `*bool`, rather than a `bool`, so that callers of `Store.PrepareStagedLayer()` and `Store.ApplyStagedLayer()` can either control the value of the option or pass `nil` to use the value from the configuration file.

* The second commit adds the actual implementation, for both the "tar" extraction path (`extractTarFileEntry()`) and the "chunked" path (`chunkedDiffer.ApplyDiff()`).
 
  Rather than adding 2 more arguments to `extractTarFileEntry()`'s already long signature, this commit replaces 3 of its existing arguments and the 2 new ones with a single `*TarOptions` argument.  It also changes the `handleLChmod()` signature to take a single `mode` argument (which is called `mask` in `extractTarFileEntry()`), instead of duplicating the `StripSUIDSGID` and `ForceMask` logic in the Linux and BSD versions of `handleLChmod()`.

  On UNIX platforms, `extractTarFileEntry()` is called in a subprocess, and its output is discarded if it succeeds.  In order to actually log the names of any files whose permissions are modified, the UNIX version of `invokeUnpack()` is changed to log all of the subprocess's output (at `INFO` level) when it succeeds.  An additional `InSubprocess` field is added to the `TarOptions` struct and set in the UNIX `invokeUnpack()`, so that `extractTarFileEntry()` knows to log file names directly to `stderr`, rather than using `logrus` when it is running in a subprocess.

* The third commit adds documentation of the option to the man page and both the main and FreeBSD sample `storage.conf` files.

* The final commit adds tests for the feature.

## AI tool use

I did use AI tools to help me create this pull request.  I was unfamiliar with both the storage library code base and the Go programming language when I began working on it, so AI tools were used for the following purposes.

* Understanding Go syntax and coding practices.

* Understanding the code base, including:

  - The steps required to pass the `strip_suid_sgid` option value down to `extractTarFileEntry()` and `chunkedDiffer.ApplyDiff()`,
  - The existence of the 2 different extraction patch, and their OS-specific functions, and
  - The public APIs (`Store.PrepareStagedLayer()` and `Store.ApplyStagedLayer()`) and the need to use a pointer field in `ApplyDiffOpts`.

* Generating the test code.  The test code in the final commit combines code that was generated with access to the feature implementation and "clean room" test code that was generated from a human-readable specification.

comments.body:
> Orthogonally, we have urgent work pending on `pkg/archive`

(The “request changes” is to prevent merging until then; as to “whether this should be done at all”, that’s more open to debate.)

Thanks for taking the time to respond.

> A `storage.conf` option looks like an _entirely_ wrong layer of abstraction for that; that would be a per-build choice — the same base image can be used for multiple child images, where one of them wants the bits stripped and another preserved.
> 
> I think this should happen at container build time (and I think something like `RUN chmod -R ug-s /` can already do that, along with either preserving the original layer, or squashing the image to never contain the SetUID bits).

The use case that I'm addressing with this is running **existing** container images  in a restricted environment (with `RestrictSUIDSGID=yes`).  A build-time options to remove unneeded privileges might be a good thing, but it doesn't address that use case.  (As you note, images need to be squashed in order to remove the SUID/SGID bits from their base layers.  I honestly don't know how difficult it is to add that to the various automated image build services out there, but I'm sure it's more complicated that just modifying a Containerfile.)

> That way, also, the image works _consistently_, it will not break only for users with this option set vs. work for everyone else.

That's a good point.  The intended use of this feature is a containerized service that runs as a dedicated service user, so it has its own image store dedicated to that service.  For example, I run my Plex Media Server container with this systemd service file (which I hope to convert to a quadlet if `User=` and `Group=` are ever fully supported).

```ini
[Unit]
Description=Plex Media Server - podman container
Wants=network-online.target
BindsTo=user@383.service
After=network.target network-online.target user@383.service
PropagatesStopTo=user@383.service

[Service]
User=podman-plex
Group=podman-plex
Environment=PODMAN_SYSTEMD_UNIT=%n
Environment=XDG_RUNTIME_DIR=/run/user/383
Environment=DBUS_SESSION_BUS_ADDRESS=unix:path=/run/user/383/bus
KillMode=mixed
Delegate=yes
Type=notify
NotifyAccess=all
SyslogIdentifier=%N
ExecStartPre=/usr/bin/podman rm -f %N
ExecStartPre=/usr/bin/podman system migrate
ExecStart=/usr/bin/podman run \
        --name %N \
        --replace \
        --detach \
        --rm \
        --network host \
        --sdnotify conmon \
        --uidmap 0:@500000 \
        --uidmap 42:@500001 \
        --uidmap 100:@500002 \
        --uidmap 65534:@500003 \
        --uidmap 1000:@500004 \
        --gidmap 0:@500000 \
        --gidmap 4:@500001 \
        --gidmap 5:@500002 \
        --gidmap 8:@500003 \
        --gidmap 42:@500004 \
        --gidmap 43:@500005 \
        --gidmap 50:@500006 \
        --gidmap 100:@500007 \
        --gidmap 101:@500008 \
        --gidmap 65534:@500009 \
        --gidmap 1000:@500010 \
        --volume /opt/mediaserver/plex-container/config:/config:Z \
        --volume /opt/mediaserver/plex-container/transcode:/transcode:Z \
        --volume /opt/mediaserver/plex-container/tls:/tls:ro,Z \
        --volume /srv/media/plex/movies:/data/movies:ro \
        --volume /srv/media/plex/tv:/data/tv:ro \
        --volume /srv/media/flac:/data/music:ro \
        --label io.containers.autoupdate=registry \
        --env PLEX_GID=1000 \
        --env PLEX_UID=1000 \
        --env TZ=America/Chicago \
        --health-start-period 2m \
        docker.io/plexinc/pms-docker:latest
ExecStopPost=/usr/bin/runcon -t container_runtime_t \
        /usr/bin/rm /dev/shm/libpod_rootless_lock_383
RemoveIPC=yes
RestrictRealtime=yes
LockPersonality=yes
ProtectClock=yes
ProtectKernelModules=yes
PrivateDevices=yes
PrivateTmp=yes
MemoryDenyWriteExecute=yes
SystemCallFilter=@system-service @mount @resources sethostname
CapabilityBoundingSet=CAP_SETUID CAP_SETGID
AmbientCapabilities=CAP_SETUID CAP_SETGID
ProtectSystem=strict
ProtectHome=tmpfs
BindPaths=/run/user/383
BindPaths=/opt/mediaserver/plex-container/.local
BindPaths=/opt/mediaserver/plex-container/config
BindPaths=/opt/mediaserver/plex-container/tls
BindPaths=/opt/mediaserver/plex-container/transcode
BindReadOnlyPaths=/opt/mediaserver/plex-container/.config
BindReadOnlyPaths=/srv/media/plex
BindReadOnlyPaths=/srv/media/flac
SystemCallArchitectures=native
UMask=0077
ProtectProc=invisible
RestrictNamespaces=user mnt pid uts ipc cgroup time net
RestrictAddressFamilies=AF_UNIX AF_INET AF_INET6 AF_NETLINK
RestrictSUIDSGID=yes
```

When used like this, the `podman-plex` user has a dedicated image cache within their home directory, so sharing images isn't an issue.

This would obviously need to be documented.  Not doing so originally was definitely an oversight.

(As I understand it, `force_mask` and `ignore_chown_errors` impose similar restrictions.)

> Orthogonally, we have urgent work pending on `pkg/archive`, and this must not merge before. (But, sadly, there’s no way to tell whether “urgent” means weeks or months.)

Is there a PR or issue where this work can be tracked?  I can't make any promises, but I might even be able to help out a bit, depending on the complexity of the work.

> The use case that I'm addressing with this is running **existing** container images in a restricted environment

vs.

> > That way, also, the image works _consistently_, it will not break only for users with this option set vs. work for everyone else.

I still think it would better fit the containers model to rebuild the images — but let’s see what other @containers/podman-maintainers think.

---

> > Orthogonally, we have urgent work pending on `pkg/archive`, and this must not merge before. (But, sadly, there’s no way to tell whether “urgent” means weeks or months.)
> 
> Is there a PR or issue where this work can be tracked?

I’m afraid not.

have you considered using `--security-opt no-new-privileges=1` to run the container?

Another alternative would be to mount the backing file system for the overlay mount to have `nosuid`

> I still think it would better fit the containers model to rebuild the images — but let’s see what other @containers/podman-maintainers think.

FWIW, I agree, but getting the producers of containers images to make that change (particularly since it's more than a simple change to a Containerfile) is going to be a never-ending battle.  My goal is to enable users (particularly myself!) to run existing containerized services with only the privileges that they actually need.

I’m mostly thinking that users who want to run applications in a (non-default!) hardened environment can rebuild images, using some kind of CI automation. Admittedly, Podman itself is not that CI automation, and I don’t know how easy / difficult that is to set up.

> have you considered using `--security-opt no-new-privileges=1` to run the container?
> 
> Another alternative would be to mount the backing file system for the overlay mount to have `nosuid`

I agree with both points. Mounting the backing fs nosuid seems a lot simpler and does not need any code changes. In addition it is more secure because a user could otherwise just podman unshare and still create these files manually.

I do not think it is worth it to take on the extra maintenance load for this feature.

Closing this, as I was able to achieve the desired effect (running a container with SUID/SGID bits in its image with systemd's `RestrictSUIDSGID=yes` option) by using the `force_mask` option in my `storage.conf`.
